record_office_terminology.land_records: Alaska DNR Recorder's Office (recording districts cover boroughs/census areas; deeds, mortgages, liens, mining claims)
record_office_terminology.probate: Superior Court (probate division handles wills, estates, guardianships; organized by judicial district, not by borough)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Marriage licenses issued by the state; filed with the Alaska Bureau of Vital Statistics; no county-level marriage license office
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1913 (territorial vital registration began; compliance improved over time; pre-1913 records from church registers, Russian Orthodox parish records, and missionary records)

burned_county_authority: Alaska has no burned boroughs or census areas. Record losses are minimal due to the state's relatively recent organizational history (most boroughs organized in the 1960s-2000s). However, some pre-territorial records were lost during the transition from Russian to American control (1867) and through deterioration in remote locations. The 1964 Good Friday Earthquake destroyed some records in Anchorage, Seward, Valdez, Kodiak, and Chenega.
administrative_notes: Alaska has 30 jurisdictions: 16 organized boroughs, 2 unified municipalities (Anchorage, Skagway), and 12 census areas (unorganized boroughs designated by the Census Bureau). Alaska was purchased from Russia in 1867, organized as a District (1884), became a Territory (1912), and achieved statehood on January 3, 1959. Most boroughs were not organized until the 1960s or later -- the state's Local Boundary Commission oversees borough formation. Unlike most states, Alaska does not have counties. Boroughs approximate county functions but with varied powers. Census areas have no organized government. The superior court system is divided into four judicial districts and handles probate, adoption, and other matters typically handled by county courts in other states. Land records are filed with the Alaska DNR Recorder's Office by recording district, which may not align exactly with borough boundaries. The Alaska State Archives in Juneau and UAF Alaska and Polar Regions Collections in Fairbanks are the two primary research repositories. Russian colonial records (1741-1867) are at the Library of Congress, NARA, and the Alaska State Archives. BIA records at NARA are essential for Alaska Native genealogy. The Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act (ANCSA, 1971) created regional and village corporations whose shareholder records may contain genealogical information.

core_repositories.description: Primary state repository holding territorial vital records (births/deaths from 1913), court records from all four judicial districts, governor's papers, territorial and state government files, and transfers from state agencies. Microfilm of Russian Orthodox parish registers. Essential starting point for Alaska genealogical research.

core_repositories.source_name: UAF Alaska and Polar Regions Collections
core_repositories.repository: University of Alaska Fairbanks, Elmer E. Rasmuson Library
core_repositories.description: Major research repository holding manuscript collections, mining company records, personal papers, photographs, oral histories, newspapers (Fairbanks Daily News-Miner, Nome Nugget, and others), maps, and Alaska Native collections. Oral history program with recordings from elders across Alaska. Complementary to the Alaska State Archives.

Federal records for Alaska including Bureau of Indian Affairs records (census rolls, school records, allotment files), federal court records, customs records, military records, Bureau of Education records, and Alaska Railroad records. Essential for Alaska Native research and federal government interactions.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including Alaska vital records (births, deaths, marriages), federal census records (1870-1950), and some church records. FamilySearch has microfilmed records at the Alaska State Archives and other repositories.

Comprehensive research guide covering Alaska's unique record types, Russian colonial records, Alaska Native genealogy, recording districts, judicial districts, and borough-specific research strategies.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Alaska newspapers. Coverage includes territorial-era papers from Juneau, Fairbanks, Nome, Sitka, and other communities. Legal notices, obituaries, mining news, and community events.

Microfilmed Alaska newspapers from the territorial and statehood eras. Comprehensive collection including small-town weeklies and community papers not available elsewhere. Also available through interlibrary loan.

native_american_and_tribal_records.source_name: Bureau of Indian Affairs -- Alaska Records at NARA
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: BIA records for Alaska Native communities including census rolls (1885-1940), school enrollment records, allotment files, agency correspondence, and village records. Organized by BIA agency (Juneau, Bethel, Fairbanks, Nome, etc.). Essential for tracing Alaska Native family lines.

native_american_and_tribal_records.source_name: Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act (ANCSA) Records
native_american_and_tribal_records.repository: BIA Alaska Regional Office / ANCSA Regional and Village Corporations
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: ANCSA (1971) enrollment records contain genealogical information used to determine eligibility for corporation shareholder status. Regional corporations (Sealaska, NANA, Calista, Arctic Slope, etc.) may have shareholder family history records. Access varies by corporation.

native_american_and_tribal_records.source_name: Alaskan Russian Church Archives -- Library of Congress
native_american_and_tribal_records.description: 401 reels of microfilm containing Russian Orthodox parish registers from Alaska parishes (1772-1936). Baptisms, marriages, burials, and confessional records. Parishes in Kodiak, Sitka, Unalaska, Kenai, Juneau, and dozens of other communities. Records are in Russian and transliterated Native names. Indispensable for Alaska Native and Creole families in communities served by the Russian Orthodox Church.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Alaska Naturalization Records -- NARA / Alaska State Archives
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA Pacific Alaska Region / Alaska State Archives, Juneau
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records from Alaska territorial courts (District Court, later U.S. District Court). Many immigrants came during gold rushes (Scandinavians, Japanese, Chinese, Filipino cannery workers) and WWII military construction. Also includes Chinese exclusion case files and Japanese internment records at NARA.

military_records.repository: NARA St. Louis / NARA Pacific Alaska Region
military_records.description: Personnel records from major Alaska military installations: JBER (Anchorage), Eielson AFB and Fort Wainwright (Fairbanks), Coast Guard Base Kodiak, and WWII-era installations across the territory. WWII Aleutian Campaign records, Alaska-Siberia Lend-Lease route records, and Alaska Territorial Guard (ATG) records. ATG was composed largely of Alaska Native volunteers.

land_and_property.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records -- Alaska
land_and_property.repository: BLM GLO Records (online) / BLM Alaska State Office
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents, homestead entries, and mining claims for Alaska. Searchable online database. Alaska was a public land state -- nearly all land was federal domain. Homesteading was available under various acts into the 1980s. Alaska Native allotments under the Alaska Native Allotment Act of 1906 are at BLM Alaska.

land_and_property.source_name: Alaska DNR Recorder's Office -- Land Records
land_and_property.repository: Alaska Department of Natural Resources, Recorder's Office
land_and_property.description: Recorded documents (deeds, mortgages, liens, mining claims, easements) organized by recording district. Recording districts may not align exactly with borough boundaries. Online index available for some districts.

vital_records.repository: Alaska Bureau of Vital Statistics / Alaska State Archives
vital_records.description: Territorial vital registration began in 1913 (births and deaths). Marriage records from the territorial period. Modern vital records from the Alaska Bureau of Vital Statistics. Historical records transferred to the Alaska State Archives. For pre-1913 vital events, use church records and Russian Orthodox parish registers.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Russian Orthodox Church Archives -- Library of Congress / OCA
church_and_cemetery.repository: Library of Congress / Orthodox Church in America
church_and_cemetery.description: Russian Orthodox parish registers are the oldest church records in Alaska, dating from the 1790s in Kodiak and 1799 in Sitka. Baptisms, marriages, burials, and confessional records. Available on 401 reels of microfilm at the Library of Congress. Cover parishes across Alaska from the Aleutians to Southeast.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Presbyterian Historical Society / Moravian Archives / Gonzaga University / Diocese of Alaska
church_and_cemetery.description: Mission records from various denominations active in Alaska: Presbyterian (1878+, Sheldon Jackson era -- Southeast and Arctic), Moravian (1885+ -- Western Alaska), Catholic/Jesuit (1880s+ -- Yukon and Kuskokwim), Episcopal (1890s+ -- Interior and Arctic), Swedish Covenant (1880s+ -- Yakutat), Quaker/Friends (1890s+ -- Northwest Arctic). Baptisms, marriages, burials, and school records.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society maintaining surname files, published indexes, and research guides for Alaska genealogy. Publications include the Alaska Genealogical Society Newsletter.

Cultural and genealogical resources for Tlingit, Haida, and Tsimshian peoples of Southeast Alaska. Maintains clan records, oral histories, and cultural documentation relevant to family history research.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Alaska has no extinct boroughs, but several census areas have been renamed, reorganized, or split. Notable changes: Wade Hampton Census Area renamed to Kusilvak Census Area (2015). Valdez-Cordova Census Area split into Chugach and Copper River Census Areas (2019). Skagway-Hoonah-Angoon Census Area split into Skagway Municipality and Hoonah-Angoon Census Area (2007). Wrangell-Petersburg Census Area split into Wrangell City and Borough and Petersburg Borough (2008, 2013). Prince of Wales-Outer Ketchikan Census Area reorganized as Prince of Wales-Hyder Census Area (2008). Researchers must be aware of predecessor names when searching for historical records and census data.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Alaska has no burned boroughs or census areas. Most jurisdictions were organized in the mid-to-late 20th century, well after the era of courthouse fires that affected older states. Some records were lost during the 1964 Good Friday Earthquake (especially in Anchorage, Seward, Valdez, Kodiak, and Chenega) and during the transition from Russian to American administration (1867). Remote communities have experienced record loss through deterioration, fire, and flooding over the decades, but these losses are not systematic.
